an4.24:1.1: On one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāketa in Kāḷaka’s park.
an4.24:1.4: “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him.
an4.24:2.1: “Monks, whatever in this world with its devas, Māras & Brahmās, in this generation with its contemplatives & brahmans, its rulers & commonfolk, is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, pondered by the intellect: That do I know.
an4.24:3.1: Whatever in this world with its devas, Māras & Brahmās, in this generation with its contemplatives & brahmans, its rulers & commonfolk, is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, pondered by the intellect: That I directly know.
an4.24:3.2: That has been realized by the Tathāgata, but in the Tathāgata it has not been established.
an4.24:4.1: “If I were to say, ‘I don’t know whatever in this world… is seen, heard, sensed, cognized… pondered by the intellect,’ that would be a falsehood in me.
an4.24:5.1: If I were to say, ‘I both know and don’t know whatever in this world… is seen, heard, sensed, cognized… pondered by the intellect,’ that would be just the same.
an4.24:6.1: If I were to say, ‘I neither know nor don’t know whatever in this world… is seen, heard, sensed, cognized… pondered by the intellect,’ that would be a fault in me.
an4.24:7.1: “Thus, monks, the Tathāgata, when seeing what is to be seen, doesn’t suppose an (object as) seen. He doesn’t suppose an unseen. He doesn’t suppose an (object) to-be-seen. He doesn’t suppose a seer.
an4.24:7.4: “When cognizing what is to be cognized, he doesn’t suppose an (object as) cognized. He doesn’t suppose an uncognized. He doesn’t suppose an (object) to-be-cognized. He doesn’t suppose a cognizer.
an4.24:7.5: Thus, monks, the Tathāgata—being the same with regard to all phenomena that can be seen, heard, sensed, & cognized—is ‘Such.’
an4.24:8.1: There is no other ‘Such’ higher or more sublime.
an4.24:8.2: “Whatever is seen or heard or sensed
an4.24:8.3: and fastened onto as true by others,
an4.24:8.4: One who is Such—among the self-fettered— would not further claim to be true or even false.
an4.24:9.1: “Having seen well in advance that arrow
an4.24:9.3: —‘I know, I see, that’s just how it is!’—
an4.24:9.4: there’s nothing of the Tathāgata fastened.”
